title: Add per-reviewer timing visibility in final review panel logs
description:
## Goal

Add granular per-reviewer observability to the final review panel so operators can diagnose bottlenecks, amendment attribution, and convergence behavior.

## Problem

When a final_review panel runs (2+ reviewers with consensus threshold), runtime logs only show panel-level events. There is no visibility into when each individual reviewer starts/finishes, how long each takes, which reviewer produced which amendments, or whether reviewers run in parallel or sequentially.

This makes it impossible to diagnose whether one reviewer is a bottleneck, whether amendments come from one specific backend, or whether the panel is oscillating between reviewer opinions across rounds.

Observed on bead tanksim-6e5.2.2 (quick_dev flow): final review panel averaged ~19 min per round with a 2-reviewer setup. After 5+ rounds of approved reviews but continued amendments, there was no way to determine which reviewer kept generating amendments.

## Changes Required

### 1. Per-reviewer journal events
Add `reviewer_started` and `reviewer_completed` journal events within the final review panel execution, including:
- Reviewer backend family and model ID
- Reviewer role (e.g. final_reviewer, completer)
- Duration (for completed events)
- Outcome (amendments proposed, count)

### 2. Amendment attribution
Include per-reviewer attribution in `AmendmentQueued` events — which reviewer proposed each amendment.

### 3. Per-reviewer timing in run tail
Add per-reviewer timing to `run tail --logs` output so operators can see reviewer-level progress in real time.

### 4. Verbose mode for panel internals
Consider adding a `--verbose` flag to `run tail` that shows panel-internal details (reviewer starts, completions, individual durations).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Journal contains per-reviewer start/complete events with backend, role, duration
- Amendments are attributable to specific reviewers in journal events
- `run tail --logs` shows per-reviewer timing
- Existing panel behavior and consensus logic unchanged
- Tests cover new journal event types

## References

- GitHub issue: https://github.com/douglaz/ralph-burning/issues/77

title: Phase 1: milestone planning and bead-driven execution in Ralph Burning
description:
Context:
Ralph already has a strong execution engine, a staged requirements system, durable project/run state,
and automation/worktree infrastructure. What it lacks is a larger planning-and-routing layer that can
reason about a whole body of work, decompose it into explicit beads, and then drive existing Ralph
execution one bead at a time.

Goal:
Adapt Ralph to a flywheel-style workflow while staying close to the current system. The milestone is
the new umbrella unit. One current Ralph project/run becomes the execution container for one bead
(user-facing term: task). The canonical graph lives in `.beads/` and is managed through br/bv.

Phase-1 intent:
- add a milestone layer above the current project/run substrate
- extend full requirements into a milestone planner
- export milestone work into beads/epics in `.beads/`
- select the next bead with bv/br
- execute each bead through a milestone-aware quick_dev loop
- reconcile review outcomes back into `.beads/`
- keep execution sequential for the first release

Guardrails:
- do not build a duplicate internal task graph for phase 1
- do not replace the quick_dev workflow shape
- do not make sprints the default execution abstraction
- do not force a global internal rename from project -> task before behavior is proven
- do not attempt parallel bead execution in the first release

## Success Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- the end-to-end path exists from idea to milestone plan, bead export, next-bead selection, task execution, milestone-aware review, and bead reconciliation
- `.beads/` remains the canonical work graph while the existing Ralph project-run substrate stays in use for one active bead task at a time
- phase 1 remains sequential and deliberately stops short of parallel bead execution

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
Why this epic exists:
The point of this effort is not to bolt a new orchestration system beside Ralph. It is to give Ralph
a larger planning horizon while preserving the parts that already work: current requirements stages,
the quick_dev execution loop, durable run history, and the existing runtime/worktree substrate.

What future us should remember:
1. The milestone layer is the new abstraction; the current project/run is still the execution substrate.
2. br/bv are the source of truth for bead-space in phase 1.
3. Review must be whole-plan-aware so it does not keep asking for work that already belongs to later beads.
4. New bead creation must be intentionally conservative.

title: Define phase-1 vocabulary, invariants, and acceptance boundaries
description:
Purpose:
Capture the shared language and architectural cut lines for the integration before implementation fans out.

This epic exists because the current Ralph codebase still uses `project` as its main durable execution unit,
while the new user-facing model introduces `milestone` as the umbrella body of work and `task` as the
execution of a single bead. Without explicit rules, teams will mix the terms and accidentally design the
wrong abstractions.

Deliverables:
- glossary for milestone / task / bead / epic / project
- phase-1 invariants and non-goals
- map of current Ralph modules to future responsibilities
- release acceptance checklist for the integrated behavior

## Success Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- canonical vocabulary, guardrails, module mapping, and the cutover checklist are all captured for the phase-1 rollout
- implementation beads can reference this epic for naming, scope, and acceptance boundaries instead of inventing local rules

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
This epic is intentionally meta and front-loaded. The goal is to prevent architectural drift while several
implementation tracks proceed in parallel.

title: Publish the glossary and naming rules for milestone, task, bead, epic, and project
description:
Goal:
Write the canonical terminology for the new system.

Must define:
- milestone = umbrella unit containing one plan and many beads
- bead = canonical work item in `.beads/`
- task = one Ralph-managed execution of one bead
- epic = grouped bead in `.beads/`
- project = legacy/internal execution substrate retained during transition

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- user-facing docs and CLI language prefer milestone/task terminology
- internal compatibility rule explains when `project` still appears
- docs make clear that one current Ralph project ~= one task execution container

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
Naming is not cosmetic here. If we fail to separate milestone from task, we will either overgrow the old
project abstraction or accidentally create a second execution substrate.

title: Write the phase-1 architecture guardrails and explicit non-goals
description:
Goal:
Record the rules that keep the first implementation small, coherent, and compatible with current Ralph.

Must include:
- use br/bv directly; do not build a duplicate internal task graph in phase 1
- reuse quick_dev shape instead of inventing a new bead workflow
- keep execution sequential initially
- treat review as milestone-aware, not sprint-oriented
- create new beads only when fix-now and planned-elsewhere are both wrong
- keep current project/run substrate internally until the new naming is proven

Contract hardening notes:
- name the authoritative source of truth for milestone status and progress when `status.json`, lineage entries, journal history, and `.beads/` disagree
- settle whether task-run completion mutates an existing lineage row or appends a new terminal row, and require later beads to preserve that model
- document the phase-1 single-writer / locking assumption and the minimum recovery guarantee if a process dies mid-write
- state the compatibility policy for legacy lineage data that lacks `run_id` or uses older multi-row shapes

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- a short design note exists and is referenced by major implementation epics
- guardrails are reflected in milestone planner, review logic, and controller code
- the design note explicitly covers authoritative state, the append-vs-update completion model, partial-write repair, single-writer assumptions, and legacy compatibility
- major 2.x / 5.x / 8.x beads can reference these rules instead of redefining storage and replay semantics locally

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
This issue is the brake pedal. When implementation gets exciting, this is the issue that reminds us what
*not* to build yet.

title: Map current Ralph modules to their milestone-era responsibilities
description:
Goal:
Produce a code-aware responsibility map so implementation tasks stay grounded in the current repo.

Expected mappings:
- `requirements_drafting` -> milestone planner
- `project_run_record` -> task execution substrate with milestone/bead linkage
- `workflow_composition` -> milestone-aware quick_dev execution/review
- `automation_runtime` -> future home for parallel milestone dispatch, but not replaced in phase 1
- new `milestone_*` context -> milestone storage/state/orchestration
- `cli/*` -> milestone/task command surface and compatibility shims

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- the map points at concrete directories/files under `src/contexts/*`, `src/cli/*`, and docs
- every major implementation epic references the owning modules

comments.author: planner
comments.text: This is the issue that keeps the plan Ralph-native instead of drifting into an abstract greenfield design.

title: Write the phase-1 acceptance criteria and cutover checklist
description:
Goal:
Turn the integration goal into a concrete checklist that can drive conformance, manual smoke, and release decisions.

Must cover:
- milestone planning success
- bead graph creation and sync behavior
- next bead selection
- task creation from bead context
- milestone-aware review classifications
- conservative new bead creation
- successful bead close and advancement to the next bead

Contract hardening notes:
- add explicit release gates for state authority, retry/idempotence, partial-write repair, and review routing behavior
- treat unresolved storage/replay/reconciliation contract questions as cutover blockers even if the happy path works

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- checklist is specific enough to drive tests and signoff
- checklist clearly distinguishes phase-1 done from later parallelism work
- the checklist includes pass/fail criteria for authoritative-state decisions, restart safety, and milestone-aware review approval behavior

title: Conformance, integration tests, docs, and operator hardening for phase 1
description:
Purpose:
Turn the new design into something operable and releasable.

This epic covers:
- unit and integration tests
- conformance scenarios
- operator docs for milestone + br workflows
- explicit documentation of deferred parallelism

## Success Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- unit, integration, conformance, and operator-facing documentation cover the phase-1 milestone system at the right depth
- deferred parallelism is documented as future work without pulling implementation into phase 1

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
The controller, planner, and review changes cross many modules. This epic exists so the final system is not only
implemented, but also testable, operable, and explainable.

title: Add unit tests for milestone models, planners, prompt generation, and adapters
description:
Goal:
Verify that each subsystem's unit test coverage is sufficient by writing gap-coverage tests
for any model/service boundary not already exercised by the specialized test beads (2.7, 3.7,
4.6, 5.6, 6.5, 7.6, 8.7).

Scope:
This bead is a sweep, not a rewrite. It runs AFTER the specialized test beads land and fills
gaps they missed. Typical gaps:
- Edge cases in milestone model serialization not covered by 2.7
- MilestoneBundle contract validation edge cases not covered by 3.7
- Adapter error paths not hit by 4.6
- Prompt template boundary conditions not hit by 6.5

Non-goals:
- Do not re-test what specialized beads already prove
- Do not add integration or e2e tests (those live in 10.2 and 2.7)

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: Gap-coverage tests exist for model/service boundaries not exercised by specialized test beads (2.7, 3.7, 4.6, 5.6, 6.5, 7.6, 8.7). Each gap test documents which specialized bead was checked and why the gap exists. Total unit test coverage across all subsystems reaches the right depth for phase-1 launch.

title: Add an end-to-end scenario: idea -> milestone plan -> bead graph -> task run -> bead close
description:
Goal:
Prove the full integrated happy path once, end to end.

Scope boundaries:
- this scenario is the successful bead-close path from idea through milestone execution
- it should not wait on the separate new-bead-creation policy to land before the base system can be exercised

Contract hardening notes:
- assert that replaying the successful reconciliation boundary does not create duplicate close, journal, or lineage evidence

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- the scenario covers milestone planning, bead export, next-bead selection, task creation, quick_dev execution, successful reconciliation, and bead close
- the scenario asserts that the happy path does not create unexpected new beads
- the scenario is automated and deterministic enough to rerun during development
- the end-to-end scenario includes at least one controlled restart around reconciliation and still converges without duplicate mutations

comments.author: planner
comments.text: This is the closest thing to a reality check for phase 1. If this scenario is weak, the architecture probably is too.

title: Assemble the scenario-specific temp workspace with .beads and milestone artifacts for integration testing
description:
Goal:
Use the shared test infrastructure from 10.6 (mock adapters, fixture builders, temp workspace
helper) to create one reproducible fixture workspace for the end-to-end milestone scenario.

The fixture must include:
1. A .ralph-burning/milestones/ directory with a pre-created milestone in planning state
2. A .beads/ workspace with a small but realistic bead graph (root epic, 2-3 tasks with deps)
3. Pre-populated plan artifacts (plan.md, plan.json) from the milestone planner
4. Mock br/bv adapter configurations that return deterministic responses
5. A clean git-like state so the scenario can test sync behavior

Key constraints:
- Must be self-contained: no dependency on developer's real repo state
- Must use the shared builders from 10.6, not ad-hoc setup code
- Fixture creation must be fast (<1 second) for test iteration speed
- Fixture state must be realistic enough that the full scenario (create→plan→export→select→run→close)
  can exercise real code paths, not just stubs

Non-goals:
- Running the scenario assertions (that is 10.2.2)
- Building the test infrastructure itself (that is 10.6)

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: Fixture includes milestone artifacts and a beads workspace with realistic graph. Tests run without depending on developer's real repo state. Setup reuses shared test infrastructure from 10.6. Fixture creation completes in under 1 second. Fixture state is sufficient for the full e2e scenario path.

title: Script the end-to-end acceptance scenario and assertions
description:
Goal:
Turn the fixture into a meaningful automated happy-path scenario.

Assertions should verify:
- milestone artifacts are created and populated
- the selected bead is claimed and linked to a Ralph task
- the active task completes and reconciles through the normal success path
- milestone status and bead state advance without spawning surprise work

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- assertions cover milestone creation, bead export, selection, task creation, task completion, successful reconciliation, and status updates
- the scenario verifies the active bead is closed and the next-step signal is recorded after success
- the scenario verifies that no unexpected new bead is created on the happy path
- failures surface enough log and state context to diagnose which phase broke

comments.author: planner
comments.text: Implementation requirement: the e2e scenario script must capture structured log output from the system under test at each phase. On assertion failure, the script must dump: (1) the structured log lines from the failing phase, (2) the filesystem state of .ralph-burning/ and .beads/ at that point, and (3) a clear description of what was expected vs what happened. The script should produce a human-readable summary suitable for debugging by an operator who did not write the test.

comments.author: codex
comments.text: Boundary note: this bead owns the scenario steps and domain assertions for the end-to-end acceptance flow. The reusable runner/reporting/log-preservation harness lives in ralph-burning-9ni.10.7 so scenario logic and execution/reporting infrastructure can evolve independently.

title: Add conformance coverage for milestone-aware quick_dev review behavior
description:
Goal:
Promote the milestone-aware review scenarios into a release-facing conformance gate so this behavior stays owned by the phase-1 release track.

Scope:
- take the review scenarios proven in 7.6 and make sure the conformance suite or release check runs them as a named gate
- keep fix-now, planned-elsewhere, propose-new-bead, and approval-with-existing-later-work behavior visible in release readiness
- make failures identify which routing or approval path regressed

Key constraint:
- 7.6 owns the underlying scenario logic; this bead owns release-track adoption and conformance visibility rather than duplicating scenario definitions

Non-goals:
- defining the workflow scenarios themselves (covered by 7.6)
- the end-to-end happy path runner (covered by 10.2 and 10.7)

Contract hardening notes:
- add conformance coverage for approval despite later-bead work, approval despite non-blocking cleanup, and escalation of true contract gaps

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
The release-facing conformance suite executes or imports the 7.6 review scenarios instead of duplicating them. Fix-now, planned-elsewhere, propose-new-bead, and approval-with-existing-later-work behavior remain visible in phase-1 release readiness. Conformance failures clearly identify which classification or approval path regressed.
- conformance failures clearly separate misrouted blocker findings from harmless informational findings that should not reopen the bead

title: Document operator workflow for br sync, milestone runs, recovery, and review outcomes
description:
Goal:
Write the runbook future operators will actually need.

Must cover:
- normal milestone planning, export, next, and run flow
- br sync expectations and when import or recovery is required
- recovery after git pull, stale bead state, sync conflicts, or failed task run
- meaning of review classifications and controller states
- when a new bead is expected vs suspicious

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- the runbook covers the normal milestone plan/export/next/run flow with concrete command examples and expected controller outcomes
- the runbook explains when to use br sync --flush-only versus recovery or import-oriented steps after git pull, stale state, or malformed bead data
- the runbook documents retry, needs_operator, and successful-close paths using the actual controller and reconciliation behavior from the implemented beads
- the runbook explains fix-now, planned-elsewhere, and propose-new-bead classifications, including when new bead creation is expected versus suspicious
- docs are specific enough to unblock an operator who did not build the system

title: Capture the deferred phase-2 parallel execution design on top of existing worktree leases
description:
Goal:
Preserve the phase-2 parallel direction without letting it leak into phase-1 implementation.

This bead is a boundary note, not the full parallel-execution design.

Must cover:
- the invariants phase-1 sequential code must preserve so later parallelism stays possible
- one worktree lease per active bead task as the baseline constraint
- which questions are intentionally deferred to 11.1 and 11.2 instead of being solved here
- how br readiness and bv guidance constrain future multi-bead execution

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- a compact design note exists for the deferred parallel direction and explicitly points forward to 11.1 and 11.2
- the note names the phase-1 invariants future parallel work must not break, including lease ownership, explicit claim state, and durable resume evidence
- the note explains how phase-1 sequential code can evolve without breaking the worktree-lease model
- implementation remains explicitly out of scope for this bead

comments.author: planner
comments.text: This is intentionally a follow-on design capture, not part of the phase-1 critical path.

title: Build shared test infrastructure: mock br/bv adapters, fixture builders, and log capture helpers
description:
Goal:
Create a reusable test infrastructure module that all test beads can depend on, eliminating
duplicated setup code and providing consistent test patterns across the milestone system.

Components:

1. Mock br adapter:
   - Implements the same typed interface as the real br adapter
   - Returns configurable responses for create, update, close, dep add, sync, etc.
   - Tracks all calls for assertion (verify what mutations were requested)
   - Can simulate errors (command not found, malformed output, sync failure)
   - Configurable latency for timeout testing

2. Mock bv adapter:
   - Implements the same typed interface as the real bv adapter
   - Returns configurable next-bead recommendations, triage summaries
   - Can simulate empty graph, blocked graph, single ready bead, multiple ready beads
   - Tracks all calls for assertion

3. Milestone fixture builders:
   - Builder-pattern API for creating realistic milestone state in temp directories
   - Supports pre-populating journal events, task linkage, plan artifacts
   - Bead graph fixtures for creating .beads/ state with configurable epics, tasks, deps

4. Log capture helpers:
   - Capture tracing output during test execution
   - Assert on structured fields (e.g., assert that milestone_id and operation appear in logs)
   - Capture and display logs on test failure for debugging
   - Compatible with both unit tests and integration tests

5. Temp workspace helper:
   - Creates a complete temp workspace with .ralph-burning/ and .beads/ directories
   - Configurable initial state (empty, with milestones, with beads, etc.)
   - Automatic cleanup on drop
   - Provides paths for all expected directories

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- Mock adapters exist for br and bv operations with full call tracking
- Fixture builders can create realistic milestone/bead state in one call
- Log capture can assert on specific structured fields
- Temp workspace helper creates valid directory structures
- All helpers are documented with usage examples in doc comments
- Test infrastructure is in a dedicated module (e.g., tests/support/ or src/test_support/)
  accessible from all test files

title: Add e2e test runner with step-by-step logging, timing, and human-readable result reports
description:
Goal:
Wrap the e2e acceptance scenario in a test runner that captures detailed execution traces,
produces human-readable reports, and integrates with CI pipelines.

Requirements:
- each major phase is reported as a named step with timing and pass/fail state
- failure output includes the relevant structured logs and filesystem snapshot
- the runner emits both human-readable text and machine-readable JSON
- the runner reuses QA6 helpers and the QA10.2.2 scenario instead of creating a second scenario definition

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
1. Running the e2e suite produces a clearly formatted step-by-step report.
2. Each step shows pass-fail with timing.
3. Failed steps include relevant log output and filesystem state.
4. The report is readable by an operator unfamiliar with the system.
5. CI can interpret the result through exit code and JSON summary.
6. The runner can complete a full pass in under 2 minutes on a standard machine.

title: Backlog: parallel milestone execution on top of existing worktree leases
description:
Purpose:
Track the explicitly deferred work needed to run multiple ready beads in parallel later.

This epic is here so future-us does not forget the intended direction, but it should stay out of the phase-1
execution path.

## Success Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- deferred design work names the constraints for safe multi-bead execution on top of existing worktree leases
- these backlog items stay out of the phase-1 execution path until the sequential controller is proven

comments.author: planner
comments.text: Keep this epic deferred until sequential milestone execution is proven.

title: Design controller concurrency around one worktree lease per active bead task
description:
Goal:
Define how the milestone controller will scale to multiple active bead tasks without shared-worktree chaos.

Scope:
- assign one worktree lease to each active bead task
- define lease acquisition, renewal, release, and cleanup semantics
- define controller ownership and crash-recovery behavior for abandoned or interrupted worktrees
- show how active bead state maps to lease and worktree records without violating the sequential invariants proven in phase 1

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- the design fits the current automation_runtime lease model
- lease acquisition, renewal, release, and cleanup semantics are explicit for each active bead task
- ownership and crash-recovery semantics are explicit for interrupted runs and abandoned worktrees
- the design explains how controller state maps bead-task identity to worktree or lease ownership without hidden coordination

title: Design multi-bead selection and capacity rules using bv guidance
description:
Goal:
Define how future parallel execution chooses multiple independent beads safely.

Scope:
- extend the sequential 8.2 selection and readiness-validation logic into multi-bead choice rather than bypassing it
- define independence rules so the controller does not claim beads that are blocked, conflicting, or likely to collide operationally
- define capacity rules and operator override points for how many beads may run at once
- explain how bv planning or triage outputs inform parallel choice without becoming the sole authority

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- the design explains how sequential selection and readiness validation are lifted into safe multi-bead selection
- independence rules cover dependency conflicts, readiness, and operational collision risks before multiple beads are claimed
- capacity rules define how many beads may run concurrently, when the controller should back off, and where operator override is allowed
- the design explains how bv planning, triage, or capacity-oriented outputs inform parallel choice without bypassing controller validation
- deferred until sequential controller behavior is stable

title: Add milestone domain model, storage, and status tracking
description:
Purpose:
Introduce the new umbrella unit above the existing project/run substrate.

The milestone is the durable holder of:
- the original request
- the refined plan
- milestone-level status
- bead/task lineage
- milestone journal/history

This epic should create the storage/service/query layer that lets the rest of the system talk about a
larger body of work without breaking the current project-run model.

## Success Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- milestone state, status, lifecycle, and bead-task linkage can be created, updated, and inspected durably under `.ralph-burning/milestones`
- milestone storage remains manually inspectable and safe enough for operator recovery and later controller use

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
Phase-1 note:
Keep the milestone store beside existing `.ralph-burning/projects/` data. Do not try to migrate the
project substrate out from under the current system.

title: Define the milestone record schema and filesystem layout under .ralph-burning/milestones
description:
Goal:
Define the canonical on-disk shape for milestone state.

Expected artifacts:
- `milestone.toml`
- `plan.md`
- `plan.json`
- `status.json`
- `journal.ndjson`
- `task-runs/` or equivalent lineage mapping

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- schema is versioned and explicit about milestone identity, status, plan hash/version, active bead, and progress
- storage sits cleanly beside existing project storage
- layout is simple enough to inspect and debug manually
close_reason: Implemented milestone record schema (MilestoneRecord, MilestoneSnapshot, MilestoneProgress, MilestoneJournalEvent, TaskRunEntry) and filesystem layout (.ralph-burning/milestones/{id}/) with service ports and FS adapter implementations. All 11 tests pass.

title: Implement milestone store and service layer for create/load/update/journal operations
description:
Goal:
Build the code path that persists and mutates milestone state.

Scope:
- load/save milestone records
- append milestone journal events
- read/write plan artifacts
- maintain task/bead linkage records
- keep writes atomic enough to match existing Ralph durability expectations

Likely target areas:
- new `src/contexts/milestone_*`
- adapters/fs integration
- CLI-facing service methods

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- milestone state can be created and updated without touching `.beads/` yet
- service layer exposes the minimum operations needed by planner, controller, and CLI
close_reason: Implemented milestone service layer with create/load/update/journal/plan-persist/bead-tracking operations. 5 integration tests against FS adapter. Full CRUD lifecycle works.

comments.author: planner
comments.text: Keep this domain boring and durable. The controller and planner should depend on it, not reimplement it.

title: Add milestone status aggregation and progress queries
description:
Goal:
Compute milestone progress in a way that is useful for both CLI output and controller logic.

Must answer:
- how many beads exist
- how many are open/in progress/done/blocked
- which bead is active
- which Ralph task/run currently owns that bead
- whether the milestone is complete, blocked, or awaiting operator input

Contract hardening notes:
- define which fields are stored versus derived and what wins when milestone state disagrees with `.beads/` or task-run history
- specify how the active bead / current task / current run is selected when multiple attempts exist for one bead
- spell out how paused, failed, blocked, and needs-operator states map into milestone status outputs and who owns each distinction

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- query surfaces exist for `milestone show` / `milestone status`
- progress is derived from milestone state plus `.beads/` reads, not chat memory
- query semantics document precedence rules, latest-versus-history selection, and the paused/failed/blocked distinctions
- tests cover stale `.beads` reads, multiple attempts for the same bead, and divergent milestone-versus-run state

title: Track bead-to-task run linkage inside milestone state
description:
Goal:
Record which Ralph task/project/run attempted which bead, with enough detail to audit retries and outcomes later.

Must capture:
- milestone_id
- bead_id
- current task/project id
- run id if available
- outcome summary
- prompt version or plan hash if relevant

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- milestone state can answer 'what happened to bead X?' without reconstructing from unrelated artifacts
- controller can update linkage after each run outcome

title: Add milestone lifecycle transitions and journal events
description:
Goal:
Implement milestone lifecycle state transitions with journal event emission so the system
can track a milestone from planning through completion.

Lifecycle states:
- planning: requirements pipeline is active, plan not yet finalized
- ready: plan is complete, beads are exported, execution can begin
- running: controller is actively executing beads
- paused: execution was stopped by user or error, can be resumed
- completed: all beads are closed, milestone is done
- failed: unrecoverable error, requires operator intervention

Transition rules:
- planning → ready (plan finalized, beads exported)
- ready → running (controller starts execution)
- running → paused (user interrupt or needs_operator)
- running → completed (all beads closed)
- running → failed (unrecoverable error)
- paused → running (user resumes)
- Invalid transitions are rejected with a clear error message

Journal events:
- Each transition emits: {timestamp, from_state, to_state, actor, reason, metadata}
- Events are appended to the milestone journal (NDJSON format from 2.2)
- Metadata varies by transition (e.g., completed transitions include bead count and duration)

Key constraints:
- Transitions must be atomic: the state file and journal event are written together
- Invalid transitions must produce descriptive errors (not just "invalid state")
- The transition logic must be testable in isolation (no filesystem dependency in unit tests)

Non-goals:
- The controller state machine (covered by 8.1 — controller states are internal to the controller)
- Bead-to-task linkage (covered by 2.4)
- Status aggregation queries (covered by 2.3)

Contract hardening notes:
- distinguish milestone lifecycle from controller-internal state and list the allowed or forbidden combinations
- define replay and repair semantics when the journal append and status snapshot update do not succeed together
- state whether lifecycle transitions are always explicit service calls or can be inferred from bead/task outcomes

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
All valid lifecycle transitions are implemented and produce journal events. Invalid transitions are rejected with descriptive error messages. Transitions are atomic (state file and journal event written together). Transition logic is testable in isolation without filesystem. Journal events include timestamp, from/to state, actor, reason, and metadata.
- transition rules cover the controller-state boundary, snapshot-plus-journal consistency, and restart safety after partial transition writes
- tests prove that paused, failed, blocked, and needs-operator are not silently coerced into one another

title: Add structured tracing spans and log fields for milestone, controller, and bead operations
description:
Goal:
Instrument all milestone-facing code paths with structured tracing so operators can debug
and monitor the system effectively.

Must cover:
- Milestone CRUD operations (create, load, update, delete)
- Lifecycle state transitions (planning → ready → running → completed, etc.)
- Journal event appends and reads
- Controller operations (bead selection, claim, execution start/stop, reconciliation)
- br/bv adapter command invocations (command, arguments, duration, exit code)
- Bead graph mutations (create, close, dependency add, sync)

Structured fields (used consistently across all spans):
- milestone_id
- bead_id
- task_id (project/run id)
- operation (human-readable operation name)
- stage (for workflow operations)

Requirements:
- Use the tracing crate with structured fields (tracing::info_span!, tracing::instrument)
- Follow existing Ralph tracing patterns from other contexts
- All error paths must log the full error chain with context
- Performance-sensitive paths should include timing spans
- Log levels should be meaningful: ERROR for failures, WARN for degraded, INFO for operations, DEBUG for internals

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- All milestone-facing code paths emit structured traces
- Log output can be filtered by milestone_id, bead_id, or task_id
- Error paths include enough context for debugging without source code access
- A RUST_LOG=ralph_burning::contexts::milestone=debug style filter works correctly
- Controller loop iterations produce a clear trace showing what was decided and why

title: Add integration tests for milestone store CRUD, journal, lifecycle, and linkage
description:
Goal:
Prove the milestone store implementation with comprehensive integration tests that exercise
the full API surface on real filesystem state.

Test categories:

1. Milestone CRUD:
   - Create a new milestone and verify on-disk artifacts (milestone.toml, status.json, journal.ndjson)
   - Load an existing milestone and verify all fields round-trip correctly
   - Update milestone fields and verify persistence
   - List milestones in a workspace
   - Handle missing/corrupted milestone directories gracefully

2. Journal events:
   - Append events and verify NDJSON format
   - Read events in chronological order
   - Verify event schema (timestamp, actor, event type, payload)
   - Handle truncated/corrupted journal files

3. Lifecycle transitions:
   - Test all valid transitions (planning → ready → running → completed, etc.)
   - Test that invalid transitions are rejected with clear errors
   - Verify journal events are emitted for each transition
   - Test transition preconditions (e.g., cannot transition to running without an active bead)

4. Bead-to-task linkage:
   - Record a task run against a specific bead
   - Query which task ran which bead
   - Handle multiple attempts (retry history)
   - Verify linkage survives milestone reload

5. Recovery:
   - Verify atomic writes do not produce partial state
   - Recover from interrupted writes where possible
   - Verify temp directory isolation between tests

All tests must:
- Use temporary directories (no shared filesystem state)
- Produce clear assertion messages on failure
- Use the test infrastructure helpers from QA6 where available
- Run in under 5 seconds total

Contract hardening notes:
- add cases for legacy lineage shapes, duplicate terminal rows, interrupted multi-file writes, and replay after a partially applied completion
- verify recovery when milestone state, journal history, and task-run lineage drift and then have to be reconciled on reload

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- Full CRUD coverage for milestone store
- All lifecycle transitions tested (valid and invalid)
- Journal persistence tested with schema validation
- Bead linkage CRUD tested
- Error conditions produce clear error messages
- Tests are self-contained and parallelizable
- integration coverage includes legacy-data tolerance, partial-write repair, duplicate-prevention on replay, and multi-attempt same-bead histories

title: Extend requirements_drafting into a milestone planner
description:
Purpose:
Turn the current full requirements pipeline into the source of truth for milestone-scale planning.

Instead of ending only in a single project seed, the full pipeline should produce a versioned milestone bundle
containing the umbrella plan, acceptance framing, workstreams, and bead-generation instructions.

This is where the large-context reasoning belongs. The implementation loop should consume the output, not re-do it.

## Success Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- `requirements_drafting` can emit a MilestoneBundle plus durable plan artifacts suitable for bead export
- existing quick requirements and project-seed flows remain coherent during rollout

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
Planner principle:
do the hard reasoning once in plan space while the whole milestone still fits in context. The rest of the system
should operate on the resulting explicit structure.

title: Define a versioned MilestoneBundle contract and renderer set
description:
Goal:
Define the machine-readable and human-readable output of milestone planning.

Bundle should include:
- milestone identity/name
- executive summary
- goals and non-goals
- constraints and assumptions
- acceptance map
- workstreams/epics
- bead proposals and dependency hints
- recommended default flow for bead execution
- generated AGENTS guidance seed material

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- schema/versioning strategy is clear
- renderers can emit deterministic `plan.md` and `plan.json`
- downstream bead export has a stable input contract
close_reason: Implemented MilestoneBundle contract with versioned schema, identity, executive summary, goals/non-goals, constraints, acceptance map, workstreams with bead proposals, dependency hints, default flow, and AGENTS guidance. Renderers emit deterministic plan.md and plan.json. JSON Schema derivation via schemars. 11 bundle tests pass.

title: Extend the full requirements pipeline so it can emit a MilestoneBundle
description:
Goal:
Wire milestone planning into the existing full-mode stage machine.

Scope:
- decide whether milestone output extends `project_seed` or introduces a new final stage
- persist milestone-oriented committed stage output
- preserve current caching, rollback, and atomic stage commit behavior
- ensure question rounds still invalidate only the correct downstream artifacts

Likely target areas:
- `src/contexts/requirements_drafting/model.rs`
- `src/contexts/requirements_drafting/service.rs`
- `src/contexts/requirements_drafting/contracts.rs`
- `src/contexts/requirements_drafting/renderers.rs`

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- a full requirements run can end in a milestone bundle
- stage reuse and invalidation semantics remain coherent
- the change does not quietly break existing quick mode behavior

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
This is the beating heart of the integration. If this output is weak or unstable, the rest of the system
will compensate badly and drift back into ad-hoc chat planning.

title: Wire MilestoneBundle through the full-mode stage machine and run state
description:
Goal:
Wire the MilestoneBundle type through the full-mode requirements pipeline stage machine
and run state so that the planner can emit a structured milestone specification.

Changes:
1. Add MilestoneBundle as a recognized output type in the stage machine
2. Route milestone-mode runs through the full requirements pipeline (question rounds,
   scope refinement, plan generation) with MilestoneBundle as the terminal output
3. Store the MilestoneBundle in run state so downstream stages can access it
4. Preserve the existing stage machine transitions — milestone mode adds new output
   paths without breaking the current flow

Key constraints:
- The stage machine must cleanly distinguish between quick-mode (no MilestoneBundle) and
  full-mode milestone runs
- Run state must persist the MilestoneBundle across pause/resume cycles
- The MilestoneBundle must carry enough structure for downstream renderers (plan.md, plan.json)

Non-goals:
- Defining the MilestoneBundle contract itself (covered by 3.1)
- Rendering plan artifacts (covered by 3.3)
- Question-round invalidation semantics (covered by 3.2.2)

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: MilestoneBundle is a recognized output type in the stage machine. Milestone-mode runs route through the full pipeline to produce a MilestoneBundle. MilestoneBundle persists in run state across pause/resume. Stage machine cleanly distinguishes quick-mode from milestone-mode. Existing non-milestone pipeline behavior is preserved.

title: Preserve question-round invalidation semantics when milestone outputs change
description:
Goal:
Ensure that the question-round invalidation semantics still work correctly when the
requirements pipeline emits milestone-oriented outputs.

Current behavior:
- When a user answers a question, previously generated plan artifacts may be invalidated
  if the answer changes scope or requirements
- The stage machine re-runs affected stages from the invalidation point

New milestone-aware behavior:
1. When milestone outputs (MilestoneBundle, plan.md) exist and a question round changes scope,
   the invalidation must cascade to the MilestoneBundle and all derived artifacts
2. The invalidation should be precise: only re-run stages affected by the changed question,
   not the entire pipeline
3. If the MilestoneBundle is invalidated, downstream consumers (plan renderers, bead export)
   must detect the stale state and re-derive from the new bundle

Key constraints:
- Existing non-milestone invalidation behavior must not change
- Invalidation must be detectable by downstream stages (version or hash comparison)
- The stage machine must handle partial invalidation without corrupting run state

Non-goals:
- Changing how questions are asked or answered
- The MilestoneBundle wiring itself (covered by 3.2.1)

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: Question-round invalidation cascades to MilestoneBundle and derived artifacts when scope changes. Invalidation is precise (only affected stages re-run). Downstream consumers detect stale state via version or hash comparison. Existing non-milestone invalidation behavior is unchanged. Partial invalidation does not corrupt run state.

title: Keep requirements show/status surfaces intelligible with milestone-oriented runs
description:
Goal:
Keep the existing requirements show and status CLI surfaces intelligible when runs produce
milestone-oriented outputs instead of the traditional plan format.

Changes:
1. requirements show: when a run produced a MilestoneBundle, display milestone metadata
   (name, goal, workstream count, bead count) instead of raw plan output
2. requirements status: show pipeline progress in terms of milestone stages
   (questions, scope, planning, bundle generation) not just generic stage names
3. Backward compatibility: non-milestone runs display exactly as before

Key constraints:
- Output format changes are additive (new fields, not removed fields)
- The show command must handle both milestone and non-milestone run artifacts
- Supports --json for structured output

Non-goals:
- The milestone-specific CLI commands (covered by 9.1)
- Changing how the pipeline works internally (covered by 3.2.1)

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: requirements show displays milestone metadata for milestone-oriented runs. requirements status shows pipeline progress in milestone stage terms. Non-milestone runs display exactly as before. Supports --json for structured output. Output changes are additive.

title: Render plan.md, plan.json, and an explicit acceptance map from the milestone planner
description:
Goal:
Produce durable plan artifacts that humans and downstream code can both consume.

Deliverables:
- `plan.md` for human review
- `plan.json` for machine use
- explicit acceptance map linked to workstreams/beads

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- artifacts are deterministic for the same inputs
- they carry enough structure to drive bead creation and review scope boundaries

title: Generate workstreams, epics, bead specs, and dependency hints from the planning output
description:
Goal:
Make the planner output explicit enough that bead creation is mostly translation, not another free-form planning pass.

Must include:
- milestone root epic proposal
- child epic/workstream proposals
- bead specs with rationale, scope, and acceptance criteria
- dependency hints suitable for br dep edges
- notes about what is intentionally deferred to later beads

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- the output is strong enough for a deterministic first export into `.beads/`
- review can later recognize 'planned elsewhere' because the planner named that work explicitly

title: Create a milestone record automatically from completed planner output
description:
Goal:
Automatically create a milestone record from the completed planner output so the user
does not have to manually bridge between planning and execution.

When the requirements pipeline completes in milestone mode:
1. Extract milestone metadata from the MilestoneBundle (name, goal, workstreams, bead specs)
2. Create a new milestone record via the milestone store (from 2.2)
3. Initialize the milestone in planning state with appropriate journal events
4. Store the plan hash and source metadata for drift detection
5. Return the milestone_id so downstream operations (bead export, CLI) can reference it

Key constraints:
- Creation must be idempotent: re-running the planner for the same milestone should update,
  not duplicate
- The milestone record must reference the plan artifacts (plan.md, plan.json) by path
- If the milestone store is unavailable, fail with a clear error rather than silently skipping

Non-goals:
- The planning pipeline itself (covered by 3.2.x, 3.3, 3.4)
- Bead export to .beads (covered by 4.3)

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: Milestone record is created automatically from completed planner output. Creation is idempotent (re-running updates, not duplicates). Milestone references plan artifacts by path. Plan hash and source metadata are stored for drift detection. Store unavailability produces a clear error.

title: Preserve current quick requirements and project-seed bootstrap behavior during rollout
description:
Goal:
Introduce milestone planning without breaking today's single-project flows.

Must preserve:
- quick requirements path
- project create from requirements seed
- project bootstrap behavior
- existing docs and tests where the legacy path remains valid

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- the new milestone planner is additive or carefully gated
- legacy project-seed paths still work until explicitly replaced

title: Add requirements tests for cache reuse, invalidation, and milestone bundle handoff
description:
Goal:
Prove that the planner remains safe under the same operational conditions as the current requirements system.

Must test:
- cache reuse
- downstream invalidation
- question round resume
- milestone bundle creation
- legacy compatibility where still supported

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: - test coverage exists at model/service level and at least one CLI-visible path

title: Add br/bv integration for the canonical bead graph
description:
Purpose:
Make `.beads/` the canonical work graph for the new milestone workflow.

Ralph should not invent a second internal graph in phase 1. Instead it needs reliable adapters around:
- br for create/update/comment/dependencies/claim/close/sync
- bv for next-bead recommendations and graph-aware analysis

This epic owns that integration boundary.

## Success Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- Ralph talks to the canonical bead graph through typed br and bv adapters rather than scattered shell-outs
- milestone export, sync safety, and adapter tests make `.beads/` trustworthy for planning and execution

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
Rule of thumb:
if the state belongs to bead-space, prefer putting it in `.beads/` and talking to it through br/bv instead of
creating a competing Ralph-only representation.

title: Implement a br adapter for create/update/comments/dependencies/claim/close/sync operations
description:
Goal:
Wrap br command execution behind a typed boundary Ralph can trust.

Needed capabilities:
- create epic/task issues
- update fields and labels
- add comments
- add/remove dependencies
- claim and close beads
- sync/export/import status checks
- parse machine-readable outputs where available

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- adapter surfaces typed success/error results
- command failures are translated into actionable app errors
- controller and planner code do not manually shell out all over the codebase

comments.author: planner
comments.text:
The main thing to remember is that br is intentionally non-invasive. Export/import/sync behavior is part of the
functional contract here, not an afterthought.

title: Add process execution wrapper and structured command errors for br
description:
Goal:
Create a thin process execution wrapper that runs br commands as subprocesses and returns
typed results with structured error information.

Components:
1. Process runner: executes br as a subprocess with configurable timeout
   - Captures stdout, stderr, and exit code
   - Supports both synchronous and async execution
   - Handles command-not-found, timeout, and non-zero exit gracefully

2. Structured command errors:
   - BrNotFound: br binary not in PATH
   - BrTimeout: command exceeded timeout
   - BrExitError: non-zero exit with captured stderr
   - BrParseError: command succeeded but output was not parseable JSON
   - Each error carries enough context for logging and debugging

3. Command builder: type-safe construction of br command lines
   - Prevents argument injection
   - Supports common patterns: br show <id> --json, br update <id> --status=..., etc.
   - Provides a consistent interface for all downstream adapter operations

Key constraints:
- Must not shell-out through sh/bash — use direct process execution
- The wrapper must be testable with a mock process runner (for tests that do not need real br)
- Timeout defaults must be reasonable (e.g., 30s for reads, 60s for mutations)

Non-goals:
- Parsing br output (covered by 4.1.2)
- Mutation flows and sync (covered by 4.1.3)

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: Process runner executes br as a subprocess with configurable timeout. Structured errors cover not-found, timeout, exit error, and parse error cases. Command builder provides type-safe argument construction. No shell-out through sh/bash. Wrapper is testable with a mock process runner.
close_reason: Implemented br process wrapper with: OsProcessRunner (direct process execution, no sh/bash), BrCommand builder (type-safe arg construction with common command constructors), structured BrError enum (NotFound, Timeout, ExitError, ParseError), BrAdapter with configurable read/mutation timeouts, MockRunner trait for testing. 22 tests pass.

title: Parse and normalize br JSON output for ready/show/list/dep operations
description:
Goal:
Parse and normalize the JSON output from br commands into typed Rust structs for use
by downstream adapter operations and query models.

Must handle:
1. br show <id> --json: parse into BeadDetail (id, title, description, acceptance_criteria,
   status, priority, type, labels, dependencies, dependents)
2. br list --json: parse into Vec<BeadSummary>
3. br ready --json: parse into Vec<ReadyBead> with readiness information
4. br dep tree <id>: parse into dependency tree structure
5. br blocked --json: parse into blocked bead list with blocker information

Normalization:
- Status values are mapped to a typed enum (open, in_progress, closed, deferred)
- Priority values are parsed as integers (P0-P4)
- Dependency types are distinguished (blocks vs parent-child)
- Missing or null fields produce sensible defaults, not panics

Key constraints:
- Parser must be forward-compatible: unknown fields are ignored, not rejected
- Parse errors produce structured BrParseError with the raw output for debugging
- All structs derive Serialize for downstream JSON rendering

Non-goals:
- Process execution (covered by 4.1.1)
- Mutation operations (covered by 4.1.3)

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: Typed Rust structs exist for BeadDetail, BeadSummary, ReadyBead, and dependency tree. Status and priority values are mapped to typed enums. Unknown fields are ignored (forward-compatible). Parse errors produce structured BrParseError with raw output. All structs derive Serialize.
close_reason: Typed Rust structs for BeadDetail, BeadSummary, ReadyBead, DepTreeNode, BlockedBead with status/priority/type enums. Forward-compatible serde. 31 tests.

title: Support br mutation flows with audit-friendly logging and explicit sync calls
description:
Goal:
Support br mutation operations (create, update, close, dep add/remove, sync) with
audit-friendly logging and explicit sync discipline.

Mutation flows:
1. br create: create a new bead with title, description, type, priority, labels
2. br update: modify bead fields (status, description, acceptance_criteria, labels)
3. br close: close a bead with a reason
4. br dep add/remove: manage dependency edges
5. br sync --flush-only: flush all pending mutations to disk

Audit logging:
- Every mutation logs: operation, bead_id, arguments, success/failure, duration
- Uses structured tracing with consistent field names (bead_id, operation, outcome)
- Failed mutations log the full error chain with stderr output

Sync discipline:
- Mutations are batched: multiple operations can run before a single sync
- sync --flush-only is called explicitly at natural boundaries (not after every mutation)
- The adapter tracks whether unsync'd mutations exist (dirty flag)
- If the process crashes with unsync'd mutations, the dirty flag can be detected on restart

Key constraints:
- Mutations must be idempotent where possible (e.g., closing an already-closed bead is a no-op)
- The sync call must be logged and its success verified
- Error handling follows the structured error types from 4.1.1

Non-goals:
- Process execution (covered by 4.1.1)
- Read/query operations (covered by 4.1.2)
- Sync safety policy (covered by 4.4)

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria: Create, update, close, dep add/remove, and sync operations are supported. Every mutation is audit-logged with operation, bead_id, outcome, and duration. Sync discipline tracks dirty flag and calls sync at explicit boundaries. Failed mutations log full error chain with stderr. Sync success is verified after each flush.
close_reason: Added BrMutationAdapter with dirty tracking, mutation methods (create_bead, update_bead_status, close_bead, add_dependency, remove_dependency, sync_flush), structured audit logging via tracing, and pending mutation state query. Tests verify dirty flag semantics.

title: Implement a bv adapter for next-bead, triage, related-work, and impact analysis
description:
Goal:
Give Ralph a typed way to ask bv for graph-aware recommendations.

Needed capabilities:
- next bead selection
- triage summaries
- related-work lookups
- impact/file-overlap checks where useful
- future support for capacity/parallelization analysis

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- adapter can read the few bv commands phase 1 actually needs
- output is normalized enough for controller and prompt generator use
close_reason: BvAdapter with typed command builder for robot-triage/next/plan/priority/insights/alerts/suggest/graph. Label and recipe filters. TriageResponse, NextBeadResponse types. MockBvRunner for testing. 28 tests.

title: Materialize a MilestoneBundle into a root epic, epics, beads, and dependencies in .beads
description:
Goal:
Translate milestone planning output into actual beads managed by br.

Scope:
- create a milestone root epic
- create child epics/workstreams
- create task beads
- add dependency edges
- attach planning rationale/comments where useful
- sync the resulting `.beads/` state

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
